payload.text:
# Product Requirements Document (PRD)

## 1. Product Overview

School Result Management System aik web-based system hoga jo schools / academies ko easily student results prepare, manage aur generate karne mein madad karega. Is system ka core idea **Excel-based data entry + automated result card (PDF)** hai, jo vibe-coding approach se fast, scalable aur user-friendly banaya jayega.

System ka workflow simple hoga:

1. School Excel format download kare
2. Student & marks ka data fill kare
3. Excel upload kare
4. System automatically result calculate kare
5. Professionally designed Result Card PDF generate ho

---

## 2. Target Users

* Schools
* Academies
* Coaching Centers
* School Admin / Office Staff
* Teachers (limited access)

---

## 3. User Roles & Permissions

### 3.1 Admin (School Owner / Principal)

* School profile manage
* Classes & subjects define
* Excel format generate
* Result approve / publish
* PDF result cards download

### 3.2 Staff / Teacher

* Excel data upload
* Student marks entry
* Draft results preview

### 3.3 Student / Parent (Optional – Phase 2)

* Roll No / CNIC se result view
* PDF download

---

## 4. Core Features

### 4.1 School & Academic Setup

* School Name
* School Logo (result card pe show ho)
* Address & Contact Info
* Academic Year
* Examination Type (Mid, Final, Annual, Monthly)
* Class / Grade (e.g. Grade 1–10)

---

### 4.2 Excel Format Generator

System aik **standardized Excel file** generate karega:

**Excel Sheets Structure:**

#### Sheet 1: Student Information

* Student ID / Roll No (Required)
* Student Name
* Father Name
* Mother Name (Optional)
* Date of Birth
* Class / Grade
* Section
* Academic Year
* School Name (Auto-filled)

#### Sheet 2: Subjects & Marks

* Roll No (Linked with Student Sheet)
* Subject Name
* Total Marks
* Obtained Marks
* Passing Marks

#### Sheet 3: Meta / Instructions (Read-only)

* Instructions for filling data
* Validation rules

---

### 4.3 Excel Upload & Validation

When Excel upload ho:

* Required fields empty na hon
* Roll No duplicate na ho
* Obtained Marks ≤ Total Marks
* Marks numeric hon
* Subjects class ke sath match karen

Errors hoon to:

* Clear error message show ho
* Row & column highlight ho

---

### 4.4 Result Calculation Engine

System automatically calculate kare:

* Total Marks (All subjects)
* Obtained Marks (All subjects)
* Percentage
* Grade (A+, A, B, C, Fail – configurable)
* Pass / Fail status

**Grade Rules (Configurable):**

* A+ = 90–100%
* A = 80–89%
* B = 70–79%
* C = 60–69%
* Fail < 60%

---

### 4.5 Result Card Template

Result card professionally designed ho, jisme ye sections hon:

#### Header Section

* School Logo
* School Name
* Examination Name
* Academic Year

#### Student Information Section

* Student Name
* Father Name
* Roll No
* Class / Grade
* Section

#### Marks Table

* Subject Name
* Total Marks
* Obtained Marks

#### Summary Section

* Grand Total Marks
* Total Obtained Marks
* Percentage
* Grade
* Result Status (Pass / Fail)

#### Footer Section

* Date of Issue
* Principal / Head Signature
* School Stamp (Optional)

---

### 4.6 PDF Generation

* Individual Result Card PDF
* Bulk PDF download (ZIP)
* Print-ready A4 size
* Watermark option (School Name)

---

## 5. Non-Functional Requirements

### 5.1 Performance

* 1000+ students ka Excel upload support
* Result generation under few seconds

### 5.2 Security

* Role-based access
* Secure file upload
* Data encryption (basic level)

### 5.3 Scalability

* Multiple schools support
* Multi-session exams

---

## 6. Tech Stack (Suggested – Vibe Coding Friendly)

* Frontend: React / Next.js
* Backend: Node.js / Firebase / Supabase
* Excel Handling: SheetJS
* PDF Generation: jsPDF / PDFKit
* Storage: Cloud Storage

---

## 7. Future Enhancements (Phase 2)

* Online result portal for students
* SMS / WhatsApp result alerts
* Student performance analytics
* Certificates auto-generation
* Multi-language support (Urdu + English)

---

## 8. Success Criteria

* Excel se 0 manual re-entry
* Error-free result calculation
* Professional result card output
* School staff ka time save

---

## 9. Final Note

Ye system schools ke liye **simple, fast aur professional** solution hoga jo traditional result making ko fully automate karega aur real-world usage ke liye ready hoga.

**Developing Core System Features**

I'm currently focused on the foundational aspects of the School Result Management System. The initial setup for school/academic details is taking shape, including name, logo, address, and exam type configurations. Next, I'm prioritizing the Excel functionalities; designing and developing the export template is ongoing. I'll shortly begin the Excel import component after this.


**Validating & Calculating Results**

I'm now integrating validation for the Excel uploads, focusing on error checking. Following this, I'll dive into the result calculation engine to handle grades and pass/fail statuses. After this, designing the result card UI comes next, before I generate the results and exporting them to PDF, both individually and in bulk.

entries.description: App metadata containing the name and description of the School Result Management System.
Main HTML entry point with Tailwind CSS and Font support.
React application entry point using React 18+ createRoot API.
Global TypeScript interfaces for the application.
Business logic for calculating grades and result summaries.
Main application component with navigation and state management.
Form for setting up school profile and exam information.
Component to download a pre-formatted Excel template for data entry.
Component for uploading and parsing the Excel data file.
Dashboard for viewing and exporting results as PDF cards.
PDF generation logic using jsPDF and autoTable for result cards.

diagnostics.message: Invalid module name in augmentation, module 'jspdf' cannot be found.

payload.text:
**Investigating TypeScript Error**

I'm currently focused on a TypeScript error within `utils/pdf.ts`. The error message pinpoints line 7, citing an invalid module name during augmentation for the `jspdf` module, which the compiler can't locate. I'm investigating the import statement within the file, specifically the `jsPDF` import.


**Resolving jsPDF Augmentation**

Okay, so I'm honing in on the augmentation issue. It's looking like the fundamental problem is that TypeScript can't locate `jspdf` for augmentation. I need to make sure the import statement aligns with the library's actual import method, which is likely `import { jsPDF } from 'jspdf';`. I think I may have found the key to the solution. I'm checking now.


**Analyzing Module Resolution**

I'm now considering the common "Invalid module name in augmentation" error, especially as a senior frontend engineer. This often points to module visibility or setup issues. The standard way to integrate `jspdf-autotable` with recent TypeScript and `jspdf` versions is through direct import. If the user still needs an augmentation fix for a missing module, global types or module resolution are likely culprits. It's possible the `jspdf` version or its module export style may cause issues in this context, especially in modern setups like Vite/React.


**Refining Augmentation Strategy**

I've confirmed the import statement for `jsPDF` is correct, so I'm moving deeper into the augmentation problem, assuming that TypeScript is still having difficulty with module resolution. To fix it, if the system cannot recognize the module name, then I will consider casting the `doc` to `any`. However, the code's real issue might be that it's calling `doc.autoTable()` instead of using the function `autoTable(doc, options)`, as `jspdf-autotable` suggests. I am now examining line 7, and its usage in relation to line 88 and 106.


**Optimizing Module Imports**

I'm now thinking about how to solve the `jspdf` augmentation. I've realized that the "Invalid module name in augmentation" issue often arises from conflicts or improper module declaration. If I can refactor to use `autoTable` as a function instead of a method, it may remove the need for augmentation completely. I will try changing the calls to use `autoTable(doc, ...)` instead of `doc.autoTable(...)`, which should address the core problem. The import on line 2 probably attempts an old style auto-registration.


**Eliminating Augmentation Dependencies**

I've decided to abandon the augmentation approach entirely to resolve the module not found error, going with the functional style that is recommended by `jspdf-autotable`. By changing `doc.autoTable({...})` to `autoTable(doc, { ... })`, and importing both `jsPDF` and `autoTable` directly, I can bypass the need for augmentation and avoid type conflicts. I'm also now considering adjusting how I get the final Y. I will be replacing the current inline `@ts-ignore` to explicitly cast the doc to `any` only for accessing the `lastAutoTable`. This refactoring will greatly improve type safety and maintainability.


**Validating jsPDF Availability**

I'm now back at the `utils/pdf.ts` file, and I'm ensuring `jspdf` is available. The plan is to directly import `autoTable` from `jspdf-autotable` and replace the usage of `doc.autoTable` with the functional equivalent, `autoTable(doc, ...)`. I'll keep the direct access to the `lastAutoTable` property by type-casting as `(doc as any).lastAutoTable` as before, and verify I've completely removed the augmentation block. I'll focus on how `jspdf` is installed.


**Improving Augmentation Structure**

I'm now revising my approach to the augmentation issue in `utils/pdf.ts`, I think I've overlooked an environmental requirement. The error is likely due to how the augmentation block is combined with the import statement. It might require the augmentation to be defined in a separate `.d.ts` file or to be in a block separate from the import. I'll separate the import and augmentation to verify if that is the underlying problem, and how I can solve the issue.


**Simplifying Import Strategy**

I'm now changing the import statement to use a direct default import of `autoTable` from `jspdf-autotable`. The issue might have been caused by `jspdf` including its own types now. I'm focusing on ensuring compatibility with the user's environment in case their system expects augmentation. The line change to `autoTable(doc, {` from `doc.autoTable({` should fix the underlying module resolution problem.


**Refactoring PDF Generation**

Okay, I've confirmed that the best path forward is direct imports for both `jsPDF` and `autoTable`, using `autoTable(doc, ...)` instead of `doc.autoTable(...)`. I'm now replacing the old augmentation approach within `utils/pdf.ts`, and am making the necessary calls to `autoTable`. This directly addresses the module not found error and simplifies the structure. I'm also now considering the `@ts-ignore` for `doc.lastAutoTable`.


**Diagnosing Import Conflicts**

I'm now diving into the import strategies again, looking at the user's specific project setup. I'm focusing on why the module resolution in the augmentation block is still failing after my attempted fixes. It might be due to the combination of the `import` statement and the `declare module` block. The user may be missing a type dependency, but the real issue might be how the module is declared. I will now look at how the module resolution works in their project.


**Eliminating Unnecessary Augmentation**

I've decided to abandon the augmentation, and will use `autoTable` as a function. The main strategy involves replacing the direct method call `doc.autoTable({...})` with a functional call `autoTable(doc, { ... })`, and directly importing it. I will keep a small type cast for the `lastAutoTable` property using `(doc as any).lastAutoTable`. This looks better now.


**Analyzing Direct Module Imports**

I'm now thinking about how to handle the `lastAutoTable` property. Even with the functional `autoTable(doc, ...)` approach, it's still added to the `doc` object. I am going back to the error report and the original imports. If I remove augmentation and use the side-effect import `import 'jspdf-autotable';` along with `(doc as any).autoTable(...)`, I may resolve the error and maintain the original import style. If I import directly using `import autoTable from 'jspdf-autotable'` is better long term. The error may be because the augmentation is failing, not the import itself.


**Simplifying Type Casting**

I'm now revising my use of the `lastAutoTable` property in the `utils/pdf.ts` file. Instead of resorting to type casts, I'll attempt to directly access and use the property, if it's there. This involves directly referencing `autoTable` as a function and adjusting how I use the `lastAutoTable` property. I am going back to the documentation again, and re-reading the details. I may be missing a trick or two.

The original implementation was correct, though I will ensure that the type casting is clean, which makes the new augmentation simpler and more efficient.


**Streamlining Type Consistency**

I'll ensure I can directly access `lastAutoTable` while maintaining the functional `autoTable` approach. It's about preserving type safety through accurate use of type assertions or direct property access, and it's essential for readability and maintainability.

**Reviewing Type Safety**

I'm now back in `utils/pdf.ts`, and I'm ensuring type safety. I'm focusing on the `lastAutoTable` property access, after refactoring. I'm confirming that type assertions or direct property access work properly with the functional `autoTable` approach. It will remove the original `doc.lastAutoTable` and `@ts-ignore`, so that the typing is consistent throughout the file. I am also checking the other usages of the `doc` object.


**Prioritizing Error Resolution**

I'm now zeroing in on the original error within `utils/pdf.ts`. I have decided to focus solely on resolving the module augmentation issue. I will not make any other changes to the code, unless there is a very good reason to. I will focus only on this single issue. I am ready to implement a fix to ensure I do not modify things the user did not directly ask for. I will go back to the code.


**Addressing PDF Module Imports**

I'm now back to the `jspdf` issue. I will try a direct approach to fix the issues, and focus on the import lines in `utils/pdf.ts`, after reviewing the issue, especially line 7. I'll make sure that `autoTable` is correctly imported and used directly, and eliminate any augmentation attempts.


**Confirming Minimal Code Changes**

I'm now zeroing in on a quick fix, focusing on only the requested change. I will remove the augmentation and add a type cast, which I think is enough to solve this one particular issue. I am now going back to the code, and double-checking that this will work.



**Simplifying PDF Table**

I'm now revising my table generation. I'm focusing on directly importing `autoTable` and calling it as a function. The previous augmentation method and the inline type assertion I considered are not the best approach, so I will now refactor to remove the original error. I'm switching the line `doc.autoTable({...})` to `autoTable(doc, { ... })`.

I am now thinking about how to refactor the inline `@ts-ignore` to use `(doc as any).lastAutoTable` as before, and verify I've completely removed the augmentation block. I'm hoping I can fix this.


**Improving Bulk PDF Generation**

I'm now integrating the PDF table generation from the previous steps with the bulk PDF function. entries.description: Fixed TypeScript augmentation error by replacing 'declare module' with the recommended 'autoTable' function approach from jspdf-autotable.

payload.text:
Fix the following errors:

```
Invalid argument passed to jsPDF.f3
```

**Pinpointing The Error**

Okay, I'm now focusing on the 'Invalid argument passed to jsPDF.f3' error. It seems like the issue is with `jsPDF` itself, specifically regarding font styles, sizes, or coordinates being incorrectly passed. I'm also considering potential conflicts with the `autoTable` plugin, especially concerning version compatibility or configuration.


**Debugging Font Handling**

I've been examining the `jsPDF` code, zeroing in on font rendering and text positioning. It seems `jsPDF.f3` errors frequently surface when text alignment or font handling goes awry internally. The discrepancy between the specified version in `index.html` as `4.0.0`, which might be incorrect, is also a key suspect; it should be checked against `esm.sh` for compatibility. Focusing now on `addResultCardToDoc` and any potential type mismatches within the options object.


**Revising Dependencies & Logic**

I'm now zeroing in on dependency versions and the code's logic. The current `jspdf` version declared as 4.0.0 is likely incorrect, as the current version is 2.5.2, which must be fixed. Also, I've identified a possible issue with the `angle` property used for watermarking, it's `angle` in current versions, so this might be problematic. Also, the potential use of `(doc as any).lastAutoTable.finalY + 15;` looks like an area to scrutinize, especially if autoTable fails to run and `finalY` is undefined, leading to the error.


**Optimizing PDF Generation**

I'm now zeroing in on the PDF generation process, specifically aiming to simplify it to mitigate the `jsPDF.f3` error. I'm going to update the import map for `jspdf` to version 2.5.1 and ensure `autoTable` is initialized correctly. It's looking like `doc.text` is the culprit, especially with its `align` option; I need to ensure that alignment logic isn't generating `NaN` offsets or that fonts are correctly specified. I am investigating now, with the assumption of using `doc.text(text, x, y, options)` where `options` contains an invalid value.


**Refining Parameter Validation**

I'm now focusing on parameter validation within the PDF generation. I will investigate each `doc.text` call to ensure all inputs, such as `config.name`, `config.address`, and `result.student.rollNo`, are strings or have default values. This involves explicitly casting them using `String()` to prevent `NaN` values and the `jsPDF.f3` error. I will also check that `autoTable` is being applied and used correctly, especially regarding the dependency injection, as a failure in `autoTable` might lead to undefined values.


**Fixing Coordinate Calculation**

I'm now focusing on fixing how the `finalY` coordinate is calculated. My priority is preventing `NaN` values from entering the calculations, which can be triggered when `lastAutoTable` doesn't exist. I'm going to add a check to make sure `lastAutoTable` is valid before referencing it, ensuring a default value is assigned if it doesn't exist, preventing the `jsPDF.f3` error from being triggered. This will be done in the `utils/pdf.ts` file.

entries.description: Update jspdf version to 2.5.1 to ensure compatibility and stability.
Fix PDF generation errors by safely handling coordinates and ensuring all text inputs are strings. Added checks for NaN values which typically cause the jsPDF.f3 error.
Fix PDF generation errors by safely handling coordinates and ensuring all text inputs are strings. Added checks for NaN values which typically cause the jsPDF.f3 error.

payload.text:
Fix the following errors:

```
Invalid argument passed to jsPDF.f3
```

**Investigating a jsPDF Error**

I'm currently looking into a common jsPDF error, specifically `Invalid argument passed to jsPDF.f3`. It seems a non-numeric value has slipped through when calculating coordinates or dimensions for drawing functions such as `rect()`, `text()`, or `line()`. I'm trying to pinpoint where a non-number is entering the code during PDF generation.


**Pinpointing Potential Culprits**

I've been drilling down on potential sources of the `NaN` value causing the jsPDF error. My analysis of `utils/pdf.ts` pinpoints `finalY` calculated after `autoTable` as the prime suspect. Specifically, I'm examining how `autoTableResults.finalY` might be undefined. I'm focusing on the logic that computes `finalY` and subsequent calls to `doc.rect()` and `doc.text()` that use it. It seems a conditional that does not handle undefined or changed property names can allow a `NaN` value to flow through to drawing functions.


**Validating `finalY` Calculation**

I'm now revisiting the `finalY` calculation. The issue of potentially accessing it before the table is complete is a concern. I've realized `jsPDF-autotable` provides `autoTable` call results or `doc.lastAutoTable.finalY`. I need to ensure I'm using the correct method. I'm double-checking the `doc.lastAutoTable` property and variations in plugin setups. Also, I must ensure `finalY` defaults to a valid number. Moreover, I'm strengthening the logic around `angle` parameter when calling `doc.text`, especially considering the differences between jsPDF-autotable and jsPDF.


**Refining Coordinate Validation**

I'm now revisiting `utils/pdf.ts` to solidify the fix. I'm focusing on validating all coordinates within `addResultCardToDoc`, particularly `finalY`. To prevent NaN errors, I will ensure that the finalY is always a valid number. Moreover, I will implement checks for isNaN() for every coordinate used in drawing functions. Finally, I will ensure that the pageWidth remains valid. I'm scrutinizing `autoTableResults` for unexpected or undefined values, as these are the sources of `NaN`.


**Solidifying Defensive Programming**

I'm expanding my defensive coding approach in `utils/pdf.ts`. I'm going beyond just `finalY` and adding comprehensive checks for `NaN` within drawing functions, especially `doc.rect()`. I'm validating `pageWidth` obtained from `doc.internal.pageSize.getWidth()` to ensure it's a number and that I handle potential edge cases. This also includes examining `footerY = 270`, and handling how a hard-coded value of `270` is used.

entries.description: Implemented highly defensive coordinate handling in PDF generation to resolve 'Invalid argument passed to jsPDF.f3' errors caused by NaN or undefined values in drawing functions.

payload.text:
**Isolating the Watermark**

I'm zeroing in on the watermark removal. The `utils/pdf.ts` file seems to house the key logic, specifically within the `addResultCardToDoc` function. I've located the "Watermark" section and am now examining the `watermarkX` variable and related code, aiming for precise deletion.


**Targeting the Code**

I've isolated the specific code responsible for generating the watermark within `utils/pdf.ts`. It's the block using `doc.text` with an angle of 45 degrees, which I need to remove. I'm focusing on deleting this segment entirely to eliminate the watermark's appearance.

entries.description: Removed the watermark section from the PDF generation logic as requested.

payload.text: Add a 'Print All' button to the ResultDisplay component that generates the bulk PDF and then triggers the browser's print dialog for the generated PDF. This will streamline the process for schools wanting to print results directly. and pdf k color aur achy bna do

**Considering the "Print All" Feature**

I'm focusing on integrating a "Print All" button within the `ResultDisplay` component. My primary aim is to call the `generateBulkPDF` function and immediately initiate the browser's download/print process for the created PDF. This will simplify the user flow.


**Evolving the PDF Design**

I'm now zeroing in on PDF aesthetics. I'm modifying `generateBulkPDF` in `utils/pdf.ts` to improve colors and allow for printing. I plan to use `doc.autoPrint()` or return a data URL. I'm also working on refining the design of the result card, using deeper blues, subtle borders, and improved status indicators within the PDF's header and table sections. This includes better `autoTable` styles and refined spacing.


**Refining PDF Output and Colors**

I'm now focusing on optimizing the PDF color scheme and incorporating a "print" flag within `generateBulkPDF` in `utils/pdf.ts`. The aim is to call `doc.autoPrint()` and `window.open(doc.output('bloburl'), '_blank')`. I am updating `ResultDisplay.tsx` to include the print button and refining colors for the header. I'll test new accent blues, and crisp borders, striving for high text contrast. The plan is to set main blue to `#1e40af`, light accent to `#eff6ff`, pass to `#15803d`, and fail to `#b91c1c`.


**Revising PDF Aesthetics**

I'm now overhauling the PDF's appearance to resemble a certificate or official document. I am modifying `utils/pdf.ts` to include a colored accent bar, improved font weights, and a cleaner layout. The `generateBulkPDF` function will get a `print` parameter, and I'll use `doc.output('bloburl')` for printing. I am adding "Print All" and individual row print buttons in `ResultDisplay.tsx`, each sporting the Lucide `Printer` icon.

entries.description: Added 'Print All' button and individual 'Print' actions. Integrated with updated PDF utility for browser print dialog support.
Improved PDF aesthetics with modern colors, better spacing, and added browser print support.

payload.text: Enhance the color scheme of the generated PDF result cards to be more professional and visually appealing, using a palette that aligns with a school's branding.

**Refining the Aesthetics**

Okay, so I'm honing in on the visual upgrades for the PDF result cards. The goal is to make them pop with a professional look, keeping in mind that "professional" could mean "school branding". I'm digging into `utils/pdf.ts` again, ready to incorporate improvements inspired by what I'm learning.


**Enhancing Visuals Further**

I'm now focusing on a specific color palette using deep navy as the primary and a soft amber/gold as a secondary accent. I'm imagining a light slate background. I will implement a border or dual-color stripe for the header, and plan to use `jspdf-autotable` for improved table styling with high-contrast headers. I'm also looking at the summary box, to give it rounded corners or use shading for clarity.


**Improving Document Layout**

I'm now consolidating changes and refining the PDF layout. I will update `addResultCardToDoc` to use a more structured layout, and I'll leverage `doc.roundedRect` for the summary box. Font sizes and colors are also being tuned for better readability. I've re-reviewed `ResultDisplay.tsx` and can confirm it doesn't need significant changes based on the PRD, and will double-check for requests to enhance the PDF colors.


**Optimizing the Card Elements**

I'm now zeroing in on the finer details, and I've decided on light grey background cards or subtle dividers. The table headers will be indigo, with striped rows, and the summary box will be a modern "Card" style at the bottom, and the Grade will be emphasized in a rounded badge. I'm ready to get started on the refined `utils/pdf.ts` updates.

entries.description: Enhanced the PDF aesthetic with a modern, high-contrast professional palette. Added rounded rectangles for the summary section and refined typography for better visual hierarchy. Removed the watermark and optimized colors for 'branding' feel.

**Refining PDF Output**

I'm currently working on fixing the date of birth format within the PDF. It's appearing as a numerical value, so I need to find the source of this data and correctly render the DOB. I'm also planning to improve the visual styling of the student information section, particularly the "Roll Number" area, to enhance its overall presentation.


**Debugging Date Conversions**

I've identified the date issue: it's an Excel serial date format. To resolve this, I'm adding a utility function in `components/FileUploader.tsx` (initially thought it was an import issue). Now I will manually convert these serial numbers to DD-MM-YYYY strings. Then, I plan to improve the layout of the Student Info section, with subtle backgrounds and alignment refinements in the `drawLabelValue` layout within `utils/pdf.ts`.


**Implementing Date & Layout**

I'm now implementing the date conversion in `components/FileUploader.tsx`, addressing the Excel serial date issue and correcting date formatting. Simultaneously, I'm improving the "Student Information Section" within `utils/pdf.ts`, considering horizontal spacing and potentially a grid-based layout for a more professional look. I'll ensure that the date parsing and correction is addressed in tandem with visual refinements.

entries.description: Added robust Excel date parsing to convert numeric Excel serial dates into a human-readable DD-MM-YYYY format, ensuring the Date of Birth displays correctly.
Redesigned the student information section in the PDF with a cleaner grid layout, consistent typography, and optimized visual hierarchy for the Roll Number and other key details.
